title: Socially Responsible Finance: How to Optimize Impact
abstract: Can a socially responsible fund (SRF) improve social welfare while maximizing assets under management? We consider a two-sector model integrating financial intermediation, emissions’ negative externalities, and investors’ social preferences with regard to value alignment and impact. In scenarios with a high proportion of value-aligned investors, the SRF invests in clean sectors and compels recipients companies to use low-emission suppliers from the polluting sector, which appeals to both investor types. Alternatively, the SRF adopts a dual-fund approach, with one fund targeting clean sectors for value-aligned investors and another focusing on reducing direct emissions in polluting sectors to attract impact investors.

article.1 Model:
Issue Section: Article Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

An increasing number of investors are eager to include sustainability as a criterion in their portfolio choices. In 2022, $1 in every $8 of the $66.6 trillion United States assets under professional management was under the banner of “socially responsible” asset managers, as reported by The Forum for Sustainable and Responsible Investment.

However, this movement faces its share of skepticism. Some researchers argue that socially responsible investing is merely a marketing ploy used by financial intermediaries to lure capital from well-intentioned investors, with no real impact on social welfare.1This raises several questions: How can socially responsible capital curb the negative externalities caused by profit-driven corporations? Can a socially responsible fund (SRF) truly make a positive difference in society if its primary goal is to maximize its assets under management? Finally, investors’ moral preferences are multifaceted: Some care about owning a portfolio with a relatively low emission footprint, while others care about their impact on social welfare. How does this affect the optimal strategy of the SRF?

To answer these questions, this paper develops a competitive general equilibrium model in which firms’ production is riskless but entails negative externalities. Investors possess the capital necessary for entrepreneurs to operate firms. Capital flows from investors to entrepreneurs through financial intermediaries or funds. Investors can choose between a return-maximizing fund (RMF), which delivers the highest possible financial performance, or a socially responsible fund (SRF), which aims to maximize assets under management. Investors are concerned with the pecuniary and nonpecuniary performance of their portfolios. They differ not only in the relative weight they put on nonfinancial performance but also in the way they measure the latter. Some areimpact investors, who place value on the potential positive impact on social welfare that can be achieved through collective investment decisions. Others arevalue-alignment investors, who have a preference for portfolios with a low-emission footprint, regardless of whether this investment strategy directly contributes to improving social welfare.2Because we focus on the role of investors, entrepreneurs are not assumed to be concerned with reducing their firms’ externalities: They simply maximize the net expected profit of their firm after paying investors their due share. They face friction in the capital market, which endogenously makes them willing to comply with SRF requirements, as long as these requirements are not too costly.

For parsimony, our model assumes two sectors. Each sector produces one good using as inputs capital and the good produced in the other sector. One of the goods is produced in a “polluting sector” that generates emissions adversely affecting people’s utility. The other sector (the “clean” sector) does not have direct emissions; however, it requires the polluting sector’s good as an input. Both goods are used for consumption and production.

We identify three primary strategies an SRF can adopt to cater to the nonpecuniary preferences of investors, which we write asScope 1 strategy,Scope 3 strategy, andMere Exclusion strategy.3The Scope 1 strategy entails investing capital primarily in the polluting sector but with a commitment to exclusively finance firms that cap their direct emissions below a predetermined threshold. The Scope 3 strategy involves investing primarily in the clean sector but requires that recipient firms purchase inputs (or sell outputs to) only from polluting sector firms that cap their emissions below a predetermined threshold. The third strategy involves excluding polluting sector firms from the SRF portfolio and investing all capital in the clean sector without any further requirements from recipients of SR capital.

We first establish that mere exclusion has an impact only if the SRF size is sufficiently large, but this impact comes at the cost of a substantial deterioration in the fund’s financial performance. A small SRF opting for mere exclusion has no impact, as the polluting firms excluded from the SRF portfolio are financed with non-SR capital.4In contrast, although the first two strategies lead to very different portfolios, they can effectively reduce aggregate emissions by incentivizing firms to lower their environmental impact. The magnitude of this reduction is contingent on(1)the proportion of the targeted sector that is financed with SR capital and(2)the presence of capital market frictions in that sector. Importantly, both strategies enable the SRF to deliver financial returns that differ from those offered by the return-maximizing fund only for the cost of monitoring firms’ compliance with the emission thresholds.

The strategies also differ in the type of investor they attract. The Scope 1 strategy only appeals to impact investors, as it generates an impact but comes with a high-emission footprint portfolio. The mere exclusion strategy leading to competitive financial returns only appeals to value-aligned investors, as its zero emission portfolio has no impact. Finally, the Scope 3 strategy appeals to both types of investors, as it entails investing in clean sector firms while affecting polluting sector firms through supply chain incentives.

To solve for the equilibrium, we endogenize the size of the SRF by assuming it opts for the strategy that maximizes its assets under management, taking into account the optimal investment choice of each investor. We compare this strategy with the one that would maximize its impact on welfare.

Our main results are as follows:

First, if the market capitalization of the polluting sector is large enough compared to that of the clean sector and if the good produced in the polluting sector is mainly used as an intermediary good, then the upstream Scope 3 strategy maximizes both the SRF equilibrium size and its impact.

Second, when there are more value-aligned investors than impact investors, the Scope 3 strategy dominates, both in terms of assets raised and social impact, regardless of consumer preferences and production parameters. The reason is that the SRF attracts relatively important amounts of capital from value investors by offering them a portfolio with clean sector companies. Impact investors can then piggyback on this by pooling their forces to this fund and using the Scope 3 mechanism to have an impact. Hence, we establish complementarity between value-alignment and impact investors when the former are relatively more important in number. With the Scope 3 strategy, the SRF pools the two types of investors into a unique fund, despite their largely different nonpecuniary benefits. Pooling the two types of investors endogenously leads to a larger SRF in equilibrium and, hence, greater impact. In comparison, a Scope 1 fund would only attract impact investors. Because there are few of them, it would lead to a small SRF size and, therefore, also to little impact. Under the Scope 3 strategy, the polluting sector endogenously splits into two subsectors, one that caters to firms and produces with low emissions and one that caters to consumers (producing the same good with higher emissions and selling it at a lower price).

Third, we find that when(1)there are enough impact investors and(2)the polluting sector is relatively small and sells mostly to consumers (rather than firms), the SRF offers two separate products: a Scope 1 fund that attracts impact investors and a mere exclusion fund that attracts value-alignment investors. This strategy, separating the two types of responsible investors, maximizes size and social impact.

The critical role of supply chain emissions in achieving a net-zero global economy transition is commonly acknowledged. Using data from Trucost,Bolton and Kacperczyk (2021)provide evidence that both the average and total Scope 3 emissions of firms are greater than their Scope 1 emissions. In addition to their aggregate size, Scope 3 emissions are economically important for two reasons:

First, firms might be tempted to strategically reduce their own Scope 1 carbon emissions by outsourcing polluting activities, resulting in increased Scope 3 emissions. They might do this to avoid regulations or taxation or to please their various stakeholders. This is likely to happen if stakeholders are looking at firms’ Scope 1 emissions only, either because they do not have access to Scope 3 information or because this is what they feel responsible for.Dai et al. (2021)document the existence of a leakage effect. They find that some firms reduce their carbon footprint through emission offshoring.

However, a second effect, which our paper aims to model, goes in the opposite direction. Supply chains can positively propagate efforts to reduce emissions. Recently,Schiller (2018),Banerjee, Homroy, and Slechten (2022), andDai, Liang, and Ng (2021)showed that suppliers tend to adopt ESG standards that are in line with those of their customers, suggesting that customers can exercise pressure on suppliers to reduce their own carbon footprint. This paper explores this pressure exercised by customers on suppliers that might reflect demands by responsible investors. This is in line withBisetti, She, and Zaldokas (2023), who find that companies with strong E&S pressure from investors tend to cut ties with suppliers experiencing negative reputational events. We believe this mechanism will become even more important in the coming years as Scope 3 emissions data become more widespread. In most jurisdictions, reporting Scope 3 emissions is still not mandatory. Recent regulators have focused on rapidly requiring firms to disclose their Scope 3 emissions in a standardized manner. In March 2022, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) proposed incorporating material Scope 3 emissions into climate-related disclosures. Concurrently, the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) is seeking to require Scope 3 emissions disclosure in its forthcoming standard. Furthermore, the European Union has already enacted the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), mandating that firms present extensive sustainability-related information. Starting in 2025, large companies in Europe including those headquartered elsewhere but operating in Europe will therefore be obligated to document and disclose their Scope 3 emissions.

Our model suggests that the emergence of high-quality mandatory Scope 3 emissions might be a game changer for ESG asset managers, enabling them to offer products that appeal both to investors who want to invest in low-emission companies and those who seek to have an impact. By mandating Scope 3 emissions caps for recipients of capital, the SRF generates a positive impact, appealing to impact investors by investing in low-footprint companies, which is also appealing to value-alignment investors. We call this the “Scope 3 strategy” of the SRF.

Our paper is related to different strands of the theoretical literature. Several papers have modeled the implications of socially responsible investors. A branch of the literature extends the model inMerton (1987)to account for investors’ ESG preferences. InHeinkel, Kraus, and Zechner (2001),Pástor, Stambaugh, and Taylor (2021), andPedersen, Fitzgibbons, and Pomorski (2021), preferences are of the value-alignment type: it is assumed that responsible investors value the ESG features of their portfolio, regardless of the impact that ESG investing may have. According to this literature, the larger the fraction of responsible investors, the larger (smaller) the required return to hold brown (res. green) companies, thus increasing the cost of capital for such companies.5This may induce companies to improve their ESG performance. As in this literature, in our paper, a firm’s incentive to reduce emissions increases with the fraction of “responsible” capital in the economy.

Papers using the corporate finance approach to study the impact of investors with social preferences includeChowdhry, Davies, and Waters (2014),Hart and Zingales (2017),Morgan and Tumlinson (2019),Broccardo, Hart, and Zingales (2020),Oehmke and Opp (2024)andGreen and Roth (Forthcoming), and more recently,Edmans, Levit, and Schneemeier (2023). Closest to our work areOehmke and Opp (2024)andGreen and Roth (Forthcoming). As we do, both papers distinguish between preferences for impact and preferences for value alignment.6Oehmke and Opp (2024)modify aHolmstrom and Tirole (1997)model to show how impact investors can coordinate and provide incentives to management to improve a firm’s social performance.Green and Roth (Forthcoming)analyze the capital allocation problem of a socially responsible investor facing a set of firms that differ in their exogenously given financial and social performance. They show that SR investors’ impact results from financing the highest social value firms that would not be financed by standard investors. In an industry equilibrium model,Albuquerque, Koskinen, and Zhang (2019)explore how consumer preferences affect corporate social responsibility. In their model, corporate social responsibility investment increases a firm’s product differentiation and, hence, reduces its risk. Our focus is on the role of financial intermediation in reducing a firm’s negative externalities.

We add to this literature in several dimensions. From the methodology perspective, to our knowledge, our paper is the first to consider the general equilibrium effects of investors’ nonpecuniary preferences and to theoretically explore the supply chain effect resulting from Scope 3 investing. In most of the literature, the size of the SR capital is exogenous. In contrast, we assume that the SRF’s objective is to maximize assets under management. We show how agents’ preferences and production technology jointly affect the SRF’s equilibrium size, strategy, and impact. An important contribution of the general equilibrium approach is to establish how the effect of the SRF goes beyond the companies where it directly invests: in existing papers, SR investing affects only the social performance of firms that receive SR capital.7While these effects are also present in our paper, we add three new equilibrium effects, which we will describe next.

First, in our model, a firm’s choice of direct and indirect emissions depends on whether SR capital is invested in the firm’s sector, butnoton whether the firm is financed with responsible capital. This is because financial frictions induce all firms thatmayreceive SR capital to adapt their production plan ex ante to be eligible for SR funding. Thus, they comply even if they are eventually financed with non-SR capital. In contrast, inOehmke and Opp (2024), SR capital only affects the social performance of the firms that receive it, and inGreen and Roth (Forthcoming), there is a separation between firms that impact investors and those that standard investors choose to finance. This equilibrium result implies that the SRF might have an equilibrium impact even if standard funds and the SRF ultimately finance similar firms with equivalent Scope 1 and Scope 3 performance.

Second, we show that Scope 3 strategies, whereby the SRF pressures firms to clean their supply chain, can improve the social performance of sectors that receive no SR capital.8

Finally, we show how the availability of information on Scope 3 emissions can allow for an optimal “pooling” of impact and value alignment investors. In our model, impact and value alignment investors can both be attracted (for different reasons) to invest in a fund that invests in the nonpolluting sector while restricting the Scope 3 emissions of these firms.
We consider a competitive general equilibrium economy in which agents are atomistic and enjoy consumption but suffer from aggregate emissions generated by the production of goods. There are 2 goods,i= 1, 2, and each can be consumed or used as an input to produce the other good. Each good is produced in a sector by a continuum of competitive firms (with endogenous mass). Each firm uses as input one unit of capital, the other good, and for goodi= 1 only, releases some emissions. The population of agents comprises a mass 1 of entrepreneurs and a mass 1 of investors. All agents enjoy consuming both goods. Each entrepreneur has the skill to run a firm but lacks capital. Each investor is endowed with one unit of capital but lacks the ability to run a firm. Each investor chooses whether to invest his capital via a standard return-maximizing fund (RMF) or via a socially responsible fund (SRF).

Each entrepreneur chooses her firm’s sector. The matching between entrepreneurs and capital is subject to friction. The sequence of actions is specified as follows:9

The SRF announces its investment strategy, specifying how much it allocates to each sector and the eligibility criteria for being financed by the SRF within each sector.Each investor chooses how to allocate their capital across funds.Each entrepreneur chooses which good to produce and whether or not to comply with conditions to be eligible to receive SRF capital.Capital is matched between funds and entrepreneurs.Production and consumption occur.

Below, we will provide the details of each building block.

1.1 Technology

Within a given sector |$i \in \{ 1, 2\}$|⁠ , each single firm requires exactly one unit of capital, for example, to build a factory. The quantity y i of good i produced by a single firm from the unit of capital depends on the firm’s input quantity |$x_j \ge 0$| of good |$j \ne i$|⁠ , and for firms in Sector 1 only, the level |$e \in [0, 1]$| of direct toxic emissions the firm releases during production. Formally: $$y_1 = ex_2^{\alpha _1 } ,$$ (1) $$y_2 = x_1^{\alpha _2 } ,$$ (2) where the parameter |$\alpha _i \in (0, 1)$|⁠ . Let a firm in sector i be indexed by |$f \in [0, K_i ]$|⁠ , where K i is the (endogenous) capitalization of sector i . Then, the aggregate emission in the economy is |$E: = \int_0^{K_1 } {e_f } df$|⁠ , where e f denotes firm f ’s direct emissions.

Each firm in Sector 1 has its own direct emissions, whereas a firm in Sector 2 is associated with two other emissions: the direct emission level of the firm’s suppliers of good 1 and the direct emission level of the firm’s industrial customers who use good 2 as an input. We call the formerupstream emissionof firmf, denoted by|$e_{up, f}$|⁠, and the latterdownstream emissionof firmf, denoted by|$e_{down, f}$|⁠. We will refer to Sector 1 as the polluting sector and to Sector 2 as the clean sector.

1.2 Consumption Preferences

Individuals derive standard Cobb–Douglas utility from the consumption of both goods but suffer from the aggregate amount of emissions in the economy. Namely, given aggregate emission E , an individual’s utility from a consumption plan ( c 1 , c 2 ) is: $$u(c_1 , c_2 , E) = {{c_1^{\gamma _1 } c_2^{\gamma _2 } } \over {(1 + E)^\delta }},$$ (3) where |$\gamma _1 + \gamma _2 = 1$| and δ measure the disutility due to aggregate emissions E . 10

1.3 Goods markets

Goods are exchanged in competitive markets. Namely, we assume that if, within Sector 1, a strictly positive mass of firms choose the same level|$e \in [0, 1]$|of direct emission, these firms sell their output in a dedicated competitive market at a price that we write as|$p_1 (e)$|⁠. We refer to good 1 produced by a firm whose direct emission level iseasgood 1 of type e.Let|${\rm{{\cal E}}}$|be the endogenous set of type-dependent markets for good 1. We consider that the market of good 1 is segmented whenever|${\rm{{\cal E}}}$|is not a singleton. Market segmentation implies that type 1 good is verified before entering a specific market. A firmfin Sector 1 whose direct emissions|$e_f \notin {\rm{{\cal E}}}$|has no dedicated market for its output. We assume that such a firm can sell its production only if the market of good 1 is not segmented, as good 1’s type is not verified in this case.

1.4 Investor preferences

Conditioning their investment strategy on firms’ nonpecuniary performance, investors can invest in a standard return-maximizing fund (RMF) or a socially responsible fund (SRF). In addition to the utility derived from consumption and aggregate emissions, as expressed inEquation (3), we assume that investors may also derive utility from investing through the SRF. We model this subjective reward as a warm-glow effect, following the approach ofAndreoni (1990). Specifically, we write an investor’s idiosyncratic sensitivity to responsible investing as|$\mu \in [0, \bar \mu ]$|⁠. When an investor of typeμinvests a fractionqof their capital in the SRF, their utility inEquation (3)increases byμqA, whereArepresents the SRF’s nonpecuniary social performance.

This simple functional form for investors’ nonpecuniary benefits captures two meaningful trade-offs concerning the SRF’s financial return and its socially responsible performance. First, an investor might be willing to accept a lower-than-competitive return if the fund demonstrates adequate social performance. Second, the warm-glow effect experienced by the investor increases with the amount invested in the fund and the level of the SRF’s social performance. We assume that investors differ in the intensity of their preference for social performance,μ. In other words, the SRF’s social performance might be considered adequate by some investors but insufficient by others. Specifically, we assume thatμis uniformly distributed on the interval|$[0, \bar \mu ]$|⁠.

We consider two distinct specifications for how investors assess an SRF’s social performance, as found in the literature:impactinvestors andvalue alignmentinvestors. We assume that a fractionθof the investors have value-alignment preferences, while the remaining fraction|$(1 - \theta )$|comprises impact investors.

For impact investors, the SRF’s social performance reflects the impact the SRF has on social welfare. Let U LF denote the level of social welfare in a situation in which there is no SRF and the economy operates under laissez-faire conditions. Additionally, let U FB represent the highest attainable social welfare in a planned economy. If the presence of the SRF results in a social welfare level of U , then impact investors measure the SRF’s nonpecuniary social performance as follows: $$A_I : = {{U - U_{LF} } \over {U_{FB} - U_{LF} }} \le 1.$$ (4)

Here, the measure of impact is relative to the maximum potential increase in social welfare. Note that|$A_I \le 1$|and reaches 1 only if the SRF manages to bring the economy to the first-best level. In contrast,AI= 0 indicates that the SRF does not improve social welfare beyond the laissez-faire level. This preference for impact aligns withHart and Zingales (2017),Broccardo, Hart, and Zingales (2020),Oehmke and Opp (2024), andGreen and Roth (Forthcoming).11

Investors seeking value alignment tend to refrain from investing in companies that do not align with their personal values. This concern for value alignment is similar to that in, for example, Heinkel, Kraus, and Zechner (2001) , Pedersen, Fitzgibbons, and Pomorski (2021) , and Pástor, Stambaugh, and Taylor (2021) . In the context of our model, investors assess the socially responsible performance of the SRF based on the extent to which its portfolio emissions footprint is smaller than that of the market portfolio. Specifically, we define the emission footprint of a portfolio with sector weights |$(\omega _1 , \omega _2 )$| as |$\delta \omega _1 e$|⁠ , where e represents the average emissions of firms in Sector 1 within the portfolio. Let F M denote the emission footprint of the market portfolio. Then, the SRF’s value-alignment performance, given a portfolio emission footprint of F , is defined as follows: $$A_V = {{F_M - F} \over {F_M }} \le 1.$$ (5)

The best performance in terms of the emission footprint is achieved with a portfolio that has zero emissions, that is,F= 0, which corresponds toAV= 1.12Note that we follow the literature13in not including nonpecuniary benefits of investors into the welfare function.

1.5 Funds

Raise capital from investors and provide funding to entrepreneurs. In exchange for providing a unit of capital, a fund can require an entrepreneur to pay up to an exogenous fraction|$1 - \lambda$|of her firm’s profits.14There are two funds: a return-maximizing fund (RMF) and a socially responsible fund (SRF). The RMF maximizes its financial returns and distributes them back to its investors. To achieve this goal, it allocates its capital to the firms that generate the highest profits and systematically charges them a fraction|$1 - \lambda$|of their profits. The SRF operates differently. Its objective is to attract the maximum amount of capital, denoted byS, by offering its investors both financial returns and social performanceA.

1.6 Socially responsible compliance conditions

The SRF can commit to financing only those entrepreneurs whose firms comply with the emissions thresholds set forth by the fund. For Sector 1, this means limiting direct emissions, while for Sector 2, it involves restricting indirect emissions upstream or downstream. Compliance with these standards is required for an entrepreneur to receive financing from the SRF.

Compared to the RMF, the SRF incurs extra costs|$\psi \gt 0$|for screening and ensuring compliance among the firms that it finances. Formally, we assume that an SRF that raises a mass of capitalSfrom investors and screens a massMof firms spendsψMof the capital in screening costs and can only finance a mass of|$S - \psi M$|firms.

The strategyσof the SRF specifies (a) direct and/or indirect emission thresholds|$\{ \hat e, \hat e_{up} , \hat e_{down} \} \in [0, 1]^3$|⁠, (b) the fraction|$\lambda \prime \ge \lambda$|of the firms’ profits it leaves to the entrepreneurs it finances, and (c) the capitalSithat the SRF invests in sectori= 1, 2, where|$S_1 + S_2$|is the capital managed by the fund net of the screening cost.

1.7 Capital market friction

We assume capital allocation to entrepreneurs involves friction. The SRF’s commitment to financing compliant firms and the other fund’s indifference to emissions lead to two outcomes:(1)Compliant entrepreneurs find it easier, quicker, and more cost-effective to secure capital, while noncompliant entrepreneurs face greater challenges.(2)As the SRF’s share of total industry capital increases, noncompliant entrepreneurs in that sector encounter greater difficulty in obtaining funding. For the sake of simplicity and feasibility, we use a basic setting that delivers these properties, but it is important to note that the fundamental conclusions of our study apply to any mechanism that produces these effects.15

Let K i denote the (endogenous) aggregate amount of capital invested in sector i , and let S i be the amount of capital that the SRF invests in sector i . Thus, the resultant fraction of sector i capital from the SRF is |${{S_i } \over {K_i }}$|⁠ . The probability of an entrepreneur being financed in sector i depends on |${{S_i } \over {K_i }}$| and on the entrepreneur’s decision to comply or not. Specifically, a compliant entrepreneur is financed regardless of whether he or she is matched with the SRF or another fund. In contrast, a noncompliant entrepreneur cannot obtain financing from the SRF. We consider two extreme cases. First, a scenario with no capital market friction in a given sector means that a noncompliant entrepreneur willing to operate in that sector always manages to secure financing as long as some nonresponsible capital is invested in the sector. At the other extreme is the case of full friction, where the entrepreneur has a single random draw from the pool of investors, and thus, by not complying, has a chance of |${{S_i } \over {K_i }}$| of not receiving financing. We define |$\phi _i \left( {{{S_i } \over {K_i }}} \right)$| as the prior probability that a noncompliant firm in sector i receives financing, with $$\phi _i \left( {{{S_i } \over {K_i }}} \right): = \left\{ {\matrix{ {{\bf 1}_{\left\{ {{{S_i } \over {K_i }} \lt 1} \right\}} {\rm Frictionless capital market in sector }i} \hfill \cr {1 - {{S_i } \over {K_i }}{\rm Friction capital market in sector }i, } \hfill \cr } } \right.$$ (6) where |${\bf 1}_{\left\{ {{{S_i } \over {K_i }} \lt 1} \right\}}$| equals one if S i < K i , and 0 otherwise. In Section 6, we discuss how the model can be generalized to allow for a continuum of levels of capital market frictions.
article.2 Optimal Individual Choices:
In this section, we describe the optimal choices of consumers, entrepreneurs and investors as a function of prices and the SRF strategy. We proceed by backward induction given the sequence of action specified above.

2.1 Consumption choice

Each individualhchooses the consumption plan (c1,c2) to maximize|$u(c_1 , c_2 , E)$|⁠, subject to the budget constraint|$p_1 c_1 + p_2 c_2 \le w_h$|⁠, wherewhis the individual’s revenue, and|$p_1 : = \min _{e \in {\rm{{\cal E}}}} p_1 (e)$|⁠, implying that consumers purchase good 1 in the market where it is the least expensive. The following Lemma describes consumption choice and shows that individuals’ indirect utility is linear in their revenue.

Lemma 1. An individual h ’s demand for good |$i \in \{ 1, 2\}$| is $$c_i = {{\gamma _i w_h } \over {p_i }},$$ (7) The individual’s indirect utility is $${{\left( {{{\gamma _1 } \over {p_1 }}} \right)^{\gamma _1 } \left( {{{\gamma _2 } \over {p_2 }}} \right)^{\gamma _2 } } \over {(1 + E)^\delta }}w_h .$$ (8)

The following lemma provides a firm’s demand and supply functions as well as the firm’s profit. In general, a firm’s profit decreases with the price of its input and increases with that of its output. The profit of a Sector 1 firm also increases with its direct emissions.

Then, $$x_1 (e, p_1 , p_2 ) = \left( {{{\alpha _1 p_1 e} \over {p_2 }}} \right)^{{1 \over {1 - \alpha _1 }}} ,$$ (9) $$x_2 (p_2 , p_1 ) = \left( {{{\alpha _2 p_2 } \over {p_1 }}} \right)^{{1 \over {1 - \alpha _2 }}} .$$ (10) Let y i denote a sector- i -firm’s supply $$y_1 (e, p_1 , p_2 ) = e^{{1 \over {1 - \alpha _1 }}} \left( {{{\alpha _1 p_1 } \over {p_2 }}} \right)^{{{\alpha _1 } \over {1 - \alpha _1 }}} ,$$ (11) $$y_2 (p_2 , p_1 ) = \left( {{{\alpha _2 p_2 } \over {p_1 }}} \right)^{{{\alpha _2 } \over {1 - \alpha _2 }}} .$$ (12) The firm’s profit is $$\pi _1 (e, p_1 , p_2 ) = p_1 y_1 (1 - \alpha _1 ) = \left( {p_1 e\left( {{{\alpha _1 } \over {p_2 }}} \right)^{\alpha _1 } } \right)^{{1 \over {1 - \alpha _1 }}} (1 - \alpha _1 ),$$ (13) $$\pi _2 (p_2 , p_1 ) = p_2 y_2 (1 - \alpha _2 ) = \left( {p_2 \left( {{{\alpha _2 } \over {p_1 }}} \right)^{\alpha _2 } } \right)^{{1 \over {1 - \alpha _2 }}} (1 - \alpha _2 ).$$ (14)

2.3 Entrepreneur’s choice: Sector and technology

An entrepreneur has to choose ex ante (before raising capital and producing) the sector in which she will operate and whether or not to comply with the standards set forth by the SRF.16

LetπiCandπiNdenote the profit of a compliant and a noncompliant firm in sectori, respectively. Note that whereas a compliant firm maximizes its profit subject to direct or indirect emission caps, a noncompliant firm does not have such constraints; thus,|$\pi _{iC} \le \pi _{iN}$|⁠. In general, the ratio|${{\pi _{iC} } \over {\pi _{iN} }}$|decreases when the SRF’s emission caps are stricter.

We have shown inLemma 1, expression(8), that the indirect utility from consumption is linear in revenue. Thus, the entrepreneur’s business choices aim at maximizing her expected revenue. To fix this idea, let us focus on the case in which an entrepreneur’s revenue equals a fractionλof her firm’s profit. Considering that a noncompliant firm in sectoriis financed with probability|$\phi _i \left( {{{S_i } \over {K_i }}} \right)$|⁠, the entrepreneur’s expected revenue from running a noncompliant firm in sectoriis equal to|$\phi _i \left( {{{S_i } \over {K_i }}} \right)\pi _{iN} \lambda .$|If instead the entrepreneur chooses to comply in sectori, she is financed with certainty, and her expected revenue is|$\pi _{iC} \lambda .$|

Thus,

Lemma 3. An entrepreneur in sector i who is paid a fraction λ of its firm’s profit will choose to run a compliant firm only if $$\pi _{iC} \ge \phi _i \left( {{{S_i } \over {K_i }}} \right)\pi _{iN} .$$ (15) Inequality (15) shows that to be acceptable to an entrepreneur, the compliance condition should not reduce the firm profit π iC too much relative to π iN . Recall that if there are capital market frictions in sector i , then |$\phi _i \left( {{{S_i } \over {K_i }}} \right) = 1 - {{S_i } \over {K_i }}$|⁠ . In this case, the right-hand side of (15) decreases in |${{S_i } \over {K_i }}$|⁠ . Thus, capital market friction in sector i implies that the larger the fraction of sector i capital under SRF control, |${{S_i } \over {K_i }}$|⁠ , the smaller |${{\pi _{iC} } \over {\pi _{iN} }}$| is; that is, the tighter the emission caps that the SRF can impose on compliant firms are. If there are no capital market frictions and the sector is not fully financed with SR capital, then condition (15) becomes |$\pi _{iC} \ge \pi _{iN}$|⁠ , implying that entrepreneurs will not comply with any condition that would reduce their firm’s profit. In the absence of capital market frictions, an entrepreneur complies onlyif |${{S_i } \over {K_i }} = 1$|⁠ , that is, only if the sector is fully financed with SR capital.

2.4 Investors’ portfolio choice

Individuals are price takers, that is, they take as given the prices of goods, the funds’ financial returns, and the SRF’s social performance A . Consider an investor whose sensitivity to the SRF social performance is μ . Let q denote the fraction of his or her capital invested via the SRF. Let r denote the financial return of the RMF and r SRF denote the financial return of the SRF. Then, the investor chooses q to solve $$\mathop {\max }\limits_{q \in [0, 1]} \overbrace {\underbrace {(qr_{SRF} + (1 - q)r)}_{{\rm Investor}\prime {\rm swealth}}U}^{{\rm Utility from consumption}} + \overbrace {\mu qA}^{{\rm Warm - glow from SRF investing}},$$ (16) where |$U = {{\left( {{{\gamma _1 } \over {p_1 }}} \right)^{\gamma _1 } \left( {{{\gamma _2 } \over {p_2 }}} \right)^{\gamma _2 } } \over {(1 + E)^\delta }}$| and comes from (8) , where A = A I if the investor is an impact investor and A = A V if she is a value-aligned investor. For both types of investors, the above objective function is linear in q , and an investor prefers the SRF to the RMF only if her sensitivity μ to social performance exceeds the threshold |${{(r - r_{SRF} )U} \over A}$|⁠ . The following lemma provides the total capital invested via the SRF as a function of social welfare, the fund’s pecuniary and social performance, and the composition of the investor population in terms of impact versus value-alignment preferences.

Lemma 4. Suppose investors expect social welfare to be U , the spread between the RMF and SRF return to be |$r - r_{SRF}$| and the SRF nonpecuniary performance to be A V and A I ; then, the aggregate supply of SR capital is equal to $$\underbrace {\theta \max \left\{ {0, 1 - {{(r - r_{SRF} )U} \over {\bar \mu A_V }}} \right\}}_{{\rm Capital supplied by value - alignment investor}} + \underbrace {(1 - \theta )\max \left\{ {0, 1 - {{(r - r_{SRF} )U} \over {\bar \mu A_I }}} \right\}}_{{\rm Capital supplied by impact investors}}.$$ (17) The investment in the SRF decreases with the return spread between the RMF and the SRF and increases with the SRF nonpecuniary performance, A I and A V, and with investors’ average sensitivity to such performance, |$\bar \mu /2$|⁠ .

2.5 SRF compliance conditions

The SRF objective is to attract capital from investors whose supply is given byEquation (17). For this, it must commit to a strategy that credibly generates both pecuniary and social performance. It is straightforward to achieve value-alignment performance (AV)by avoiding investments in polluting sectors. However, impact performance (AI) requires actively reducing overall emissions, which can be achieved by inducing polluting sector firms to cap their emissions and/or by decreasing the size of the polluting sector. This has important implications for the SRF’s compliance condition. If the SRF investsSiin sectoriand requires emission caps that do not satisfy conditions(15), then no entrepreneur in that sector will comply. As a result, capitalSicannot be deployed, as there are no eligible firms, thus generating a financial return of –100%. That is, setting compliance conditions that do not meetEquation (15)is equivalent to not deployingSicapital. Section 6 and Appendix A.3 describe what occurs if the SRF does not deploy some capital. In Sections 4.3 and 4.4, we analyze emission cap strategies that satisfy condition(15)and, hence, induce compliance by all entrepreneurs of the targeted sector.

Definition 1. A competitive equilibrium is characterized as follows: A nonempty set |${\rm{{\cal E}}}$| of competitive markets for good 1. For each market |$e \in {\rm{{\cal E}}}$|⁠ : A mass |$K_1 (e) \ge 0$| of firms supplying good 1 of type e , and a mass |$K_2 (e) \ge 0$| of Sector 2 firms using as input good 1 of type e , such that $$K_i : = \sum\limits_{e \in {\rm{{\cal E}}}} {K_i } (e) \gt 0, i \in \{ 1, 2\} .$$ The aggregate supply of good 1 of type e and of good 2 is $$\eqalign{Y_1 (e): & = K_1 (e)y_1 (e, p_1 (e), p2), \cr Y_2 : & = \sum\limits_{e \in {\rm{{\cal E}}}} {K_2 } (e)y_2 (p_2 , p_1 (e)). \cr}$$ For sector i , the firms’ aggregate demand X i for good j , is $$\eqalign{ X_1 & := \sum\limits_{e \in {\rm{{\cal E}}}} {K_1 } (e)x_1 (e, p_1 (e), p_2 ); \cr X_2 (e)& : = K_2 (e)x_2 (p_2 , p_1 (e)), e \in {\rm{{\cal E}}}. \cr}$$ The consumers’ aggregate demand for good 1 of type e and of good 2 is: $$\eqalign{C_1 (e) & = \bar w{{\gamma _1 } \over {p_1 (e)}}1_{\{ e = \arg \mathop {\min }\limits_{e \in {\rm{{\cal E}}}} p_1 (e)\} } , \cr C_2 & = \bar w{{\gamma _2 } \over {p_2 }}, \cr}$$ where |$\bar w$| is individuals’ aggregate wealth.The equilibrium prices |$\{ p_1 (e)\} _{e \in {\rm{{\cal E}}}}$| and p 2 are such that markets clear: $$\eqalign{C_1 (e) + X_2 (e) - Y_1 (e) & = 0, \forall e \in {\rm{{\cal E}}}; \cr & C_2 + X_1 - Y_2 = 0; \cr}$$ Each entrepreneur correctly anticipates |${\rm{{\cal E}}}$|⁠ , the equilibrium prices of goods, and the probabilities |$\phi _1 (s_1 ), \phi _2 (s_2 )$| and chooses her firm’s sector and direct and indirect emissions to maximize her expected utility. In this section, we analyze the economy in the absence of the SRF. We focus on three cases: first, an unregulated economy where firm emissions and sector size result from laissez-faire; second, a regulated economy where the regulator can determine the caps on firm emissions, but sector sizes are endogenously determined by the entrepreneur’s choice to produce in the most profitable sector; and third, a planned economy where a social planner can dictate how to allocate capital across sectors as well as Sector 1 firms’ emissions.

article.4 Socially Responsible Investing:
We consider three types of strategies for the SRF: the mere exclusion of the polluting sector, Scope 1 investing, and upstream Scope 3 investing. For each of these strategies, we analyze the equilibrium size of the SRF, as well as its effect on social welfare. We discuss other possible strategies in Section 6.

4.1 Mere exclusion of the polluting sector

We begin by examining how simply excluding the dirty sector can enable the SRF’s portfolio to decrease the size of the polluting sector below its laissez-faire level,|$K_1^*$|⁠, thereby enhancing social welfare. We show that achieving this outcome is contingent upon the SRF being sufficiently large to starve capital in the polluting sector and produce a substantial decline in the SRF’s financial performance compared to that of the RMF. The SRF negative return spread, however, serves as a deterrent to sustainable investing. This effect is so pronounced that it can limit the size of the SRF to the point where its impact becomes negligible. This finding suggests that an SRF that solely employs exclusions has no discernible impact on social welfare.

Consider an SRF that invests all its capitalSin the clean sector without investing in the polluting sector and without requiring any emission caps to the firms it finance.20Suppose that this leads to|$K_1 \lt K_1^*$|and|$K_2 = 1 - K_1 \gt 1 - K_1^* = K_2^*$|⁠.Proposition 1implies|$\pi _2 = {{K_2^* } \over {K_2 }} \lt 1 \lt \pi _1 = {{K_1^* } \over {K_1 }}$|⁠. By favoring clean Sector 2, the supply of goods becomes scarce in Sector 1 and abundant in Sector 2 compared to the laissez-faire levels. Consequently, the equilibrium profit of Sector 1 firms surpasses that of Sector 2 firms. When|$\pi _1 \gt \pi _2$|⁠, the RMF maximizes its return by investing solely in Sector 1 and retaining the maximum fraction of its firms’ profits, offering investors a return of|$(1 - \lambda )\pi _1$|⁠. Therefore, Sector 2 can only be financed with SRF capital, requiringS=K2; hence,|$S \gt K_2^*$|⁠.

To attract entrepreneurs in Sector 2, the SRF must compensate them for the potentially higher revenues they could earn in Sector 1, where profits are higher. Thus, a fraction|$\lambda \prime$|of Sector 2 entrepreneurs’ firm profits must be left, satisfying|$\lambda \prime \pi _2 = \lambda \pi _1$|⁠. Equality is held because, in equilibrium, both goods must be produced, requiring entrepreneurs to be indifferent between producing good 1 and good 2. Notably,|$\lambda \prime = {{\pi _1 } \over {\pi _2 }}\lambda \gt \lambda$|⁠. Consequently, while the RMF invests all its capital in the high-profit Sector 1, the SRF not only invests all its capital in the low-profit Sector 2 but also retains a smaller fraction of Sector 2 firms’ relatively low profits. As a result, the SRF offers substantially smaller financial returns to its investors compared to the RMF. The return spread between the RMF and the SRF increases asK1decreases. Note that the SRF faces no screening costs because it imposes no direct or indirect emission caps on the firms it finances. Note also that if|$S \lt K_2^*$|⁠, excluding the polluting sector from the SRF portfolio has no effect on sector sizes (K1,K2), as there is enough non-SR capital to keep each sector size at its equilibrium laissez-faire level|$(K_1^* , K_2^* )$|⁠. That is, a small SRF that employs the mere exclusion strategy has no impact.

4.2 Financial cost of compliance monitoring

In the previous section, we showed that the financial performance of the SRF worsens when impact is pursued by altering the composition of the market portfolio via an exclusion strategy. For the remainder of the paper, we focus on strategies that seek to have an impact not by altering the market portfolio but rather by conditioning financing on compliance with direct or indirect emissions caps.

In this scenario, the SRF does not need to concede its entrepreneurs’ discount on the cost of capital to achieve an impact. Thus, as entrepreneurs are indifferent across the two sectors, in equilibrium, all firms generate the same profits, and funds have the same gross financial return. However, the SRF is required to allocate some of its capital to screen firms for compliance. This has two implications. First, the net return of the SRF is smaller than that of the RMF. This is because the RMF deploys its entire capital to finance firms, while the former can only invest the capital remaining after screening costs are covered. Second, because capital spent for screening cannot be utilized for financing firms, the size of the sectors is reduced.

Lemma 5. If the SRF retains a fraction |$1 - \lambda$| of the profits of the firms it finances and monitors the compliance of a mass M of firms, then The equilibrium sizes of Sectors 1 and 2 are |$(1 - \psi M)K_1^*$| and |$(1 - \psi M)K_2^*$|⁠ , respectively, and every single firm’s equilibrium profit is equal to |${1 \over {1 - \psi M}}$|⁠ . A fund’s gross return equals |${{1 - \lambda } \over {1 - \psi M}}$| independently of the composition of its portfolio, and the SRF’s return spread, net of screening cost, is $$r_{SRF} - r = - {{(1 - \lambda )\psi M} \over {(1 - M\psi )S}}.$$

4.3 Scope 1 strategy: Reducing individual firm emissions via direct investment in the polluting industry

Let us consider the case in which the SRF (a) invests primarily in the polluting sector but commits not to finance firms whose direct emissions exceed a given threshold|$\hat e$|and (b) retains|$1 - \lambda$|of the profits generated by the firms it finances. We call this SRF strategyScope 1 strategy.

To understand the effect of such an SRF strategy on the economy, letS1denote the amount of SRF capital invested in Sector 1. BecauseS1is also the mass of firms that the SRF screens for compliance, the total amount of capital invested in financing firms in the economy is|$1 - \psi S_1$|⁠.

Let us consider an entrepreneur who chooses to produce good 1. Because she chooses her firm’s emission level before searching for capital, it is optimal for her to comply and limit her firm’s direct emission to |$\hat e$| only if her expected revenue from complying exceeds that from not complying, which happens only if inequality (15) is satisfied. For the Scope 1 strategy, |$\pi _{1C} = \pi _1 (\hat e, p_1 , p_2 )$| and |$\pi _{1N} = \pi _1 (1, p_1 , p_2 )$|⁠ . Hence, condition (15) translates into $$\hat e \ge e_{dir} (S_1 ): = \phi _1 \left( {{{S_1 } \over {K_1 }}} \right)^{1 - \alpha _1 } = \phi _1 \left( {{{S_1 } \over {(1 - \psi S_1 )K_1^* }}} \right)^{1 - \alpha _1 } ,$$ (23) where the second equality follows from Lemma 5 . If |$S_1 \lt {{K_1^* } \over {1 + \psi K_1 *}}$|⁠ , then |$S_1 \lt K_1$|⁠ , meaning that some firms in industry 1 will be financed with SR capital and some with RMF capital. However, as long as |$\hat e \ge e_{dir} (S_1 )$|⁠ , all the firms in Sector 1 comply and cap their emissions at |$\hat e$|⁠ , regardless of whether they are eventually financed by the SRF or by the RMF. If there are capital market frictions, |$e_{dir} (S_1 ) = \left( {1 - {{S_1 } \over {K_1 }}} \right)^{1 - \alpha _1 }$|⁠ , which is decreasing in |${{S_1 } \over {K_1 }}$|⁠ . Thus, the larger the fraction of Sector 1 financed with responsible capital is, the tighter the direct emissions caps |$\hat e$| that the SRF can impose and, hence, the greater the impact (see panel b of Figure 2 ). If the Sector 1 capital market is frictionless, then |$e_{dir} (S_1 )$| is discontinuous, and as long as the SRF does not finance 100% of the sector, the Scope 1 strategy cannot reduce industry 1 firms’ emissions and, hence, has no impact. Independent of capital market frictions, for |$S_1 = {{K_1^* } \over {1 + \psi K_1^* }}, \;e_{dir} (S_1 ) = 0$|⁠ . That is, when fully financing the polluting sector, the SRF can use the Scope 1 strategy to cap firms’ direct emissions at any desired level |$\hat e \in [0, 1]$|⁠ . This is illustrated by the solid line in panel (b) of Figure 2 , which represents SRF A I in a frictionless Sector 1. In this case, Scope 1 has some impact only if |$S \ge K_1$|⁠ .

The amount of capital that the SRF can attract with a Scope 1 strategy depends on the fund’s pecuniary and nonpecuniary performance. ReplacingMwithS1inLemma 5, the SRF’s pecuniary performance is such that|$r_{SRF} - r = - {{(1 - \lambda )\psi S_1 } \over {(1 - \psi S_1 )S}} \lt 0$|⁠. Since the SRF portfolio consists mainly of firms from the polluting sector, its emission footprint is worse than that of the market portfolio. As a result, the SRF fails to attract value-aligned investors.

However, the SRF can attract impact investors by setting a direct emissions cap|$\hat e$|to maximize social welfare, subject to constraint|$\hat e \ge e_{dir} (S_1 )$|⁠. (Scope 1 strategy) Suppose that the SRF of size S adopts the following Scope 1 strategy : Cross-sector allocation: Priority investment in Sector 1: $$S_1 = S_{1, dir} (S): = \min \left\{ {S, {{K_1^* } \over {1 + \psi K_1^* }}} \right\},$$ and |$S_2 = S - S_1 (1 + \psi )$|⁠ . 4.4 Scope 3 strategies: Reducing individual firm emissions via the supply chain

Can the SRF induce polluting sector firms to cap their emissions by investing primarily in the clean sector? To explore this possibility, let us consider the SRF’s strategy of investing primarily in Sector 2 and conditioning said investment on some upstream emission cap|$\hat e_{up}$|⁠. The SRF has no emission requirement for Sector 1 recipients of its capital. We call this strategy theUpstream Scope 3 strategy.

This creates unique incentives for each sector. To maximize their chances of obtaining capital, Sector 2 firms comply with the SRF’s demand to produce using low-emission input. As a result, a fraction of the firms in Sector 1 are compelled to reduce their direct emissions to |$\hat e_{up}$|⁠ . This is not to pursue financing from the SRF, as either none is available in Sector 1, or it comes with no strings attached. Sector 1 firms that cap their emissions do so only because the equilibrium price of low-emission good 1, |$p_1 (\hat e_{up} )$|⁠ , is higher than that of high-emission good 1, |$p_1 <xref ref-type="disp-formula" rid="E1">(1)</xref>$|⁠ , thus compensating for the higher per-unit production cost resulting from lower direct emissions. In equilibrium, however, some firms in Sector 1 do not cap their emissions, set e = 1, and sell their output to consumers for price |$p_1 <xref ref-type="disp-formula" rid="E1">(1)</xref> \le p_1 (\hat e_{up} )$|⁠ . For this to happen, high- and low-emission firms in Sector 1 must make the same profits. From the profit Equation (13) and the fact that all Sector 1 firms face identical input prices p 2 , it must be that good 1 prices satisfy $${{p_1 <xref ref-type="disp-formula" rid="E1">(1)</xref>} \over {p_1 (\hat e_{up} )}} = \hat e_{up} .$$ (26)

Therefore, a Sector 2 firm that adheres to the SRF’s policies incurs the cost of utilizing low-emission inputs, which, in equilibrium, are more expensive than high-emission inputs. Specifically, we have |$\pi _{2C} = \pi _2 (p_2 , p_1 (\hat e_{up} ))$| and |$\pi _{2N} = \pi _2 (p_2 , p_1 <xref ref-type="disp-formula" rid="E1">(1)</xref>)$|⁠ . By utilizing Equation (13) and inequality (15) , we can determine that Sector 2 entrepreneurs expect greater revenue from compliance than from noncompliance only if the following conditions are met: $$\underbrace {\left( {{1 \over {(p_1 (\hat e_{up} ))^{\alpha _2 } }}} \right)^{{1 \over {1 - \alpha _2 }}} }_{\matrix{ {{\rm profitability }} \cr {{\rm if firm complies}} \cr } } \ge \underbrace {\phi _2 \left( {{{S_2 } \over {K_2 }}} \right)}_{\matrix{ {{\rm probability to receive capital}} \cr {{\rm iffirmdoesn}\prime {\rm tcomply}} \cr } }\underbrace {\left( {{1 \over {(p_1 <xref ref-type="disp-formula" rid="E1">(1)</xref>)^{\alpha _2 } }}} \right)^{{1 \over {1 - \alpha _2 }}} }_{\matrix{ {{\rm profitability}} \cr {{\rm if firm does not comply}} \cr } },$$ (27) where S 2 is the amount of SRF capital invested in Sector 1 and the mass of firms the SRF screens for compliance. Thus, from Lemma 5 , we have |$K_2 = (1 - S_2 \psi )K_2^*$|⁠ . By replacing (26) with (27) and solving for |$\hat e_{up}$|⁠ , one finds that the tighter upstream emission cap |$\hat e_{up}$| that the SRF can impose is $$e_{up} (S_2 ): = \phi _2 \left( {{{S_2 } \over {(1 - S_2 \psi )K_2^* }}} \right)^{{{1 - \alpha _2 } \over {\alpha _2 }}} .$$ (28)

That is, the SRF can induce all entrepreneurs in the clean sector to cap their upstream emissions to any given |$\hat e_{up} \ge e_{up} (S_2 )$|⁠ . Note that the larger |$S_2 /K_2$| is, the smaller the emissions the SRF can induce in Sector 1 by requiring upstream emission compliance in Sector 2 firms. Thus, the SRF impact increases in size S , as illustrated by the dashed line in panel c) of Figure 2 . In equilibrium, the size of Sector 1 stays at |$(1 - S_2 \psi )K_1^*$|⁠ , and the fraction of polluting sector firms that cap direct emissions to |$e_{up} (S_2 )$| is proportional to the demand from Sector 2 firms. In the following proposition, we show that the level of social welfare obtained by inducing upstream emission caps |$\hat e_{up}$| is equal to $$U_{up} (\hat e_{up} , S_2 ): = \beta {{\hat e^{\alpha _2 Z_2 } } \over {(1 + (1 - \alpha _1 )(\gamma _1 + \alpha _2 Z_2 \hat e_{up} )(1 - \psi S_2 ))^\delta }}(1 - \psi S_2 ).$$ (29)

The financial and footprint performance of the SRF do not depend on|$\hat e_{up}$|⁠. Thus, to attract impact investors, the SRF will choose|$\hat e_{up}$|to maximize|$U_{up} (\hat e_{up} )$|subject to constraint|$\hat e_{up} \ge e_{up} (S_2 )$|⁠. For the Scope 3 strategy, the equilibrium size of the SRF is equal to the investor’s supply given its pecuniary and nonpecuniary performance, and the SRF performance is that the fund delivers given its equilibrium size.

Proposition 5. Suppose that the SRF of size S adopts the following upstream Scope 3 strategy: Cross-sector allocation: Priority investment in Sector 2 with $$S_2 = S_{2, up} (S): = \min \left\{ {S, {{K_2^* } \over {1 + \psi K_2^* }}} \right\},$$ and |$S_1 = S - S_2 (1 + \psi )$|⁠ . There are no requirements for SR capital for Sector 1 recipient firms.

These firms sell their output to consumers.A mass|$\alpha _2 Z_2 (1 - \alpha _1 )(1 - \psi S_{2, up} (S))$|of Sector 1 firms that cap direct emissions to|$e = \hat e_{up} (S)$|⁠. These firms sell their output to Sector 2 firms.A mass|$K_2^* (1 - \psi S_{2, up} (S))$|of Sector 2 firms that comply with the SRF upstream emission cap|$\hat e_{up} (S)$|⁠.Each single firm in Sector 2 complies with the upstream emission cap|$\hat e_{up}$|⁠, regardless of whether it is eventually financed with SR capital.Social welfare is equal to|$\hat U_{up} (S): = U_{up} (\hat e_{up} (S), S_{2, up} (S))$|⁠.The pecuniary and nonpecuniary performances of the SRF are$$\eqalign{ r_{SRF} - r = \Delta _{up} (S)& : = - {{(1 - \lambda )\psi S_{2, up} (S)} \over {(1 - \psi S_{2, up} (S))S}}, \cr A_I = A_{I, up} (S)&: = {{\hat U_{up} (S) - U_{LF} } \over {U_{FB} - U_{LF} }}, \cr A_V = A_{V, up} (S)& : = 1 - {{S - S_{2, up} (S)} \over {SK_1^* }}. \cr}$$The SRF attracts capital from both impact investors and value-alignment investors and has an equilibrium sizeSthat solves the following:$$S = \theta \max \left\{ {0, 1 - {{\Delta _{up} (S)\hat U_{up} (S)} \over {\bar \mu A_{V, up} (S)}}} \right\} + (1 - \theta )\max \left\{ {0, 1 - {{\Delta _{up} (S)\hat U_{up} (S)} \over {\bar \mu A_{I, up} (S)}}} \right\}.$$(31)

The dotted lines in panels a) and c) ofFigure 3depict the equilibrium size of an SRF opting for the upstream Scope 3 strategy as a function of the investor’s nonpecuniary preference.

Value-alignment investors are attracted to the SRF due to its maximum performance in terms ofAVresulting from its zero-footprint approach (as shown in panel c ofFigure 2). SRF capital has some impact, which attracts impact investors. Recall that the Scope 3 upstream strategy only reduces the emissions of Sector 1 firms that sell to industrial customers. Thus, such a strategy has more impact in an economy where good 2 is mainly an intermediary good (Figure 3panels c. and d.) vs. a situation in which good 2 is equally appreciated as good 1 by consumers (Figure 3, panels a. and b.). Because the SRF’s impact performance attracts impact investors, the SRF’s equilibrium size is larger if good 2 is an intermediary good (Figure 3, panel c) than in the symmetric good economy.

The main findings of this section are summarized as follows.

The mere exclusion of Sector 1 firms from the SRF portfolio involves no screening costs; however, it can have an impact only by decreasing the weight of industry 1 in the market portfolio, which substantially deteriorates the financial performance of the SRF relative to that of the RMF.Scope 1 and Scope 3 involve screening costs that imply that the SRF return spread is negative. However, because these strategies achieve an impact without altering the composition of the market portfolio, the impact comes at a lower financial cost than for the mere exclusion strategy.The impact performance of the SRF weakly increases as the SRF size increases.A relatively small SRF can achieve some impact with the Scope 1 or Scope 3 strategies only if there is capital market friction, whereas it cannot with the mere exclusion strategy.The equilibrium size of the SRF weakly increases with investors’ average sensitivity to social performance (⁠|$\bar \mu /2$|⁠) and weakly decreases with the screening cost parameterψ.In equilibrium, all firms in the sectors targeted by the Scope 1 and Scope 3 strategies (Sector 1 and Sector 2, respectively) comply with the SRF’s emission caps, even if not all firms in these sectors are financed with SR capital.In nominal terms, aggregate wealth is independent of the level|$\hat e_{dir}$|or|$\hat e_{up}$|of emission caps enforced by the SRF. However, stricter emission caps lead to lower production levels, resulting in reduced revenue and consumption for entrepreneurs and investors in real terms and reduced aggregate emissions.
article.5 Maximizing SRF Size versus Maximizing:
In this section, we further analyze the SRF’s strategy of maximizing its equilibrium size (S) and compare it with the strategy of maximizing its impact.

Let us first recall a few key properties from the previous sections. For Scope 1 and Scope 3 strategies, first, given a specific amount of capitalSi, the emission cap (direct or indirect) that the SRF can impose by targeting sectoriis tighter when this sector’s market capitalizationKiis smaller. Second, the equilibrium capitalizationKiis proportional to|$K_i^*$|⁠, which increases withγiandαjfor|$j \ne i$|but decreases withαi. This suggests that if the difference|$\alpha _2 - \alpha _1$|is large, that is,|$K_2 \lt K_1$|⁠, targeting Sector 2 with a Scope 3 strategy might have a greater impact than targeting Sector 1 with a Scope 1 strategy. However, while the Scope 1 strategy reduces emissions for all Sector 1 firms, the upstream Scope 3 strategy reduces emissions only for Sector 1 firms that sell their output to Sector 2 firms, not those selling to consumers.22According toProposition 5, the proportion of Sector 1 firms selling to consumers whose emissions are not reduced increases withγ1. This implies that the impact of the Scope 3 strategy diminishes asγ1increases. Recalling that value alignment investors are attracted by a portfolio primarily comprising clean sector firms, whereas impact investors are attracted by a portfolio that generates an impact, we can deduce the following:

Proposition 6. If the market capitalization of the polluting sector is large enough in comparison to the clean sector market capitalization and the good produced in the polluting sector is mainly used as an intermediary good (that is, if |$\alpha _1 - \alpha _2$| and γ 1 are small enough), then the upstream Scope 3 strategy maximizes both the SRF’s equilibrium size and its equilibrium impact.

Next, we show that upstream Scope 3 strategies maximize SRF’s size and impact when investors are predominantly value-aligned. When investors are predominantly impact investors and the conditions opposite ofProposition 6are met, the SRF maximizes both the SRF size and impact by offering two products: a Scope 1 portfolio to attract impact investors and an exclusion portfolio to attract value-alignment investors.

Let us first consider the scenario in which a large fraction of investors have value alignment preferences (highθ). The Scope 1 strategy has a negativeAV,making the SRF unpalatable to value-alignment investors. Consequently, the SRF’s size is limited to|$1 - \theta$|⁠, which is insufficient to generate the impact necessary to attract impact investors. Thus, Scope 1 does not maximize the SRF’s size. In contrast, the mere exclusion strategy either has no impact and, hence, attracts only value alignment investors, or it has an impact, in which case its poor financial performance makes the SRF unpalatable to all investors. The upstream Scope 3 strategy attracts value alignment and impact investors, combining value alignment through a low portfolio footprint with an impact through upstream emission caps. Therefore, it dominates the other strategies in terms of both size and impact.

Now, let us consider the scenario in which most investors are impact investors (lowθ). To attract impact investors, the SRF needs to maximize its impact performance (AI), aligning the objective of maximizing size with maximizing impact. If the polluting sector is relatively small and produces a good mostly used as a consumption good, then the Scope 1 strategy has a greater impact than does the upstream Scope 3 strategy. Because the Scope 1 portfolio only attracts impact investors, the SRF will also offer a mere exclusion fund to attract value-alignment investors. In summary, we have:

Proposition 7. If θ is large enough, then the upstream Scope 3 strategy maximizes both the SRF’s equilibrium size and its impact on social welfare. If θ , γ 2 , and |$\alpha _2 - \alpha _1$| are small enough, then the impact-maximizing strategy is the Scope 1 strategy. To maximize its equilibrium size, the SRF offers two types of products: an impact fund adopting the Scope 1 strategy that attracts impact investors and a mere exclusion fund that does not affect the market portfolio composition but attracts value-alignment investors.

Figure 3illustratesPropositions 3,6, and7for different values of the exogenous parameters. The left panel reports the SRF’s equilibrium size, while the right panel reports the SRF’s impact performance.

Finally, asymmetries in capital market friction also play an important role. If the Sector 1 capital market is frictionless but Sector 2 is not, then targeting Sector 2 with the upstream Scope 3 strategy can maximize both size and impact for a larger range ofθ. The opposite occurs when capital market frictions are in Sector 1, but not Sector 2.
article.6 Discussion:
6.1 Downstream emission caps

Another strategy the SRF could implement would be to prioritize investing in Sector 2 but conditioning said investment on a certain downstream emission cap|$\hat e_{down}$|⁠. In Appendix A.2, we show that there is an equilibrium where all firms in Sector 2 comply, and all firms in Sector 1 cap their emissions at|$\hat e_{down}$|⁠. Given that all Sector 2 firms comply, that is, do not sell to industrial customers whose direct emissions exceed|$\hat e_{down}$|⁠, because good 2 is essential for the production of good 1, firms in Sector 1 have no alternative but to cap their direct emissions at|$\hat e_{down}$|⁠. Because all firms in Sector 1 cap their emissions, there are no high-emission industrial clients in Sector 2; hence, the compliance of these firms in Sector 2 is granted. Note that, unlike the Scope 1 and upstream Scope 3 strategies, the downstream Scope 3 strategy does not require capital market frictions to function, and the SRF has no constraint on the level of threshold|$\hat e_{down}$|it can require. As a result, downstream emission caps on Sector 2 can be a formidable instrument to reduce Sector 1 emissions, as illustrated by the dashed gray line inFigure 4. However, in actuality, this approach may be challenging for two reasons: first, Sector 2 firms must be able to control the final use of goods that have been sold, and second, Sector 1 firms must have no alternative input that could be used to replace good 2.

6.2 Degrowth

An alternative strategy that SRF could implement is not deploying a fractionρof its capitalSbut investing the rest primarily in the clean sector.23In Appendix A.3, we show that this results in a firm’s equilibrium profits that are equalized across sectors. The relative weight of each sector is laissez-faire, but the total mass of operating firms is reduced byρS. This reflects a degrowth situation in which production, consumption, and emissions are proportionally reduced by a factor|$1 - \rho S$|⁠.

We find that while such a degrowth strategy can improve social welfare, it cannot bring it to the first-best level. The degrowth strategy can potentially attract both impact investors and value-aligned investors, as it can achieve impact with a zero-emission footprint portfolio. However, it suffers from particularly high financial costs, as it wastes a fractionρof capital, leading to a return spread of|$r_{SRF} - r = - \rho r$|⁠. Overall, for the degrowth strategy to become the SRF’s equilibrium strategy, three conditions need to be met:(1)the fund must be small (otherwise, mere exclusion would dominate in both financial and nonpecuniary performance);(2)the investors must mostly be impact investors (otherwise, mere exclusion would dominate as it provides equalAVbut better return); and(3)Scope 1 and Scope 3 must not be implementable by a small fund, for example, because of an extremely high monitoring cost or because of a frictionless capital market (otherwise, a small fund using the Scope 1 or 3 strategy would achieve comparable impact with better financial performance). When one of these conditions is not met, the degrowth strategy is dominated (see the gray solid line in panel (b) ofFigure 4).

6.3 Sector-specific emission

We have focused on the simplifying assumption that only one of the two sectors has direct emissions. However, the model can be easily extended to a setting in which both sectors have direct emissions, and consumers’ utility sensitivity to such emissions is sector specific. In this situation, the SRF’s strategy does not fundamentally differ from that in our base model. Investing in the sector where direct emissions are smallest and using the Scope 3 strategy to impact the other sector would still attract both types of investors. Interestingly, when both sectors have direct emissions, the social planner’s first-best solution might require both affecting the sector’s size and capping the firm’s emissions. Also in this case, to affect the composition of the market portfolio, the SRF would have to substantially deteriorate its financial performance.

6.4 Responsible consumption

In our model, we assume that investors care about the nonpecuniary performance of their portfolio but do not consider the emission footprint of their consumption choices. For this reason, the upstream Scope 3 strategy can reduce the emissions of polluting sector firms that sell to industrial clients, but not of firms selling to consumers. We make this assumption because we believe that it is easier for individuals to opt for ESG or SR funds rather than significantly change their lifestyles to reduce their consumption emission footprints. Analyzing the role of consumer social preferences would require a comprehensive model where individuals’ social performance preferences are defined for both consumption and investment.24A simpler extension of our model would be to consider what happens if individuals are coherent in their investment and consumption choices. According to our model, this corresponds to the assumption that investors who opt for the SRF also prefer low-emission to high-emission goods when both types are available. This coherence of behavior would render the upstream Scope 3 strategy even more impactful, as the fraction of low-emission Sector 1 would increase proportionally to the volume of consumption of good 1 by socially responsible investors.

6.5 Capital market frictions

In the Internet Appendix , we describe a standard search game that leads to the following equilibrium prior probability that a noncompliant firm in sector i is financed: $$\phi _i (S_i /K_i ): = \max \left\{ {{{K_i - S_i } \over {K_i - \eta _i S_i }}, 0} \right\}$$ (32)

Here,|$\eta _i \in [0, 1]$|is a sector-specific parameter measuring the fluidity of the capital–entrepreneur matching market.

The probability|$\phi _i (.)$|is a decreasing function, reflecting the fact that it becomes more difficult for a noncompliant entrepreneur in sectorito find financing if a larger fraction of the pool of capital dedicated to this sector comes from the SRF. It is important to note that|$\phi _i (s_i )$|spans the two polar cases we have considered thus far:|$\eta _i = 1$|corresponds to the frictionless capital market, whereas|$\eta _i = 0$|is the full friction case. All the results of the paper remain qualitatively unchanged. This extension shows that an SRF’s impact on firm emissions in sectoricontinuously increases with capital market frictions|$1 - \eta _i$|in the sector.

6.6 Timing of the technology choice

We have assumed that entrepreneurs irreversibly choose their technology and business plan before searching for capital, because, to raise funds, a firm needs to provide some information to investors about the use it will make of its capital. In our model, this timing induces compliance in all firms in a sector with SRF capital, even in firms financed by nonresponsible capital. However, the resultant reduction in emissions increases with the fraction of sector capital coming from responsible investors. That is, the SRF’s impact increases with its size. However, what if firms could choose their technology after having raised their capital? In this case, within a given sector, the fraction of complying firms would reflect the fraction of the sector financed with responsible capital. As before, the SRF’s impact would still increase with the capital invested in that sector. However, within the same sector, complying and noncomplying firms coexist; thus, the main properties of our analysis still apply.
article.7 Conclusion:
This paper presents a general equilibrium model of a productive economy with negative externalities. We examine the strategies of socially responsible funds in the face of investors who value both financial returns and the fund’s social performance. We demonstrate how socially responsible funds can leverage capital market frictions and the supply chain to incentivize firms to reduce toxic emissions. We show that requiring SR capital recipients to cap their Scope 3 emissions can mitigate emissions in non-SRF-financed firms that trade with SRF-financed firms.

In our model, the SRF does not inherently prioritize social welfare. Like any financial intermediary, its primary objective is to maximize assets under management. Consequently, the SRF’s capital allocation across sectors and the pollution limits imposed on its portfolio companies depend on how investors value social performance. The Scope 3 strategy maximizes SRF size and impact under one of two conditions:(1)when most investors have a value alignment preference or(2)when the polluting sector has a large weight in the market portfolio and its output primarily serves as an intermediary good. Thus, our findings suggest that the emergence of high-quality mandatory Scope 3 emissions might be a game changer for ESG asset managers, allowing them to offer products that appeal to investors seeking to invest in low-emission companies and those seeking impact.

A.4 Micro-foundation of the Capital Matching Frictions

We consider the following matching mechanism between entrepreneurs and capital. Fix sectori. In equilibriumKiis the total amount of capital invested in the sector, and is also equal to the equilibrium mass of entrepreneurs in the sector. Let|$s_i : = S_i /K_i$|be the fraction of this capital coming from SRF. We can see the matching as the result of the following dynamic process. In every periodtentrepreneur that have not been yet finance and capital that has not been invested yet are randomly matched. Entrepreneur who are not financed intcome back to the matching market int+ 1. Capital that has not be invested intis returned to investors. A fractionsiof this capital is reinvested via SRF, the remaining|$1 - s_i$|is invested via non-socially responsible funds. We denote with|$1 - \eta _i \in [0, 1]$|the hazard rate that the capital matching process stops before the entrepreneur finds an appropriate capital provider.

Lemma 8. In every period t , an entrepreneur searching for capital is financed with probability 1 if it complies and with probability |$1 - s_i$| if she does not comply.

Proof. Let’s denote withCtthe total amount of capital not yet matched at timet. The fraction of this capital that is managed by SRF issi. LetMtdenote the mass of entrepreneurs that at beginning of timethas not been matched. Letntbe the fraction of these entrepreneurs that are not compliant. All compliant entrepreneurs can be financed with socially responsible and non-socially responsible funds, thus the first result. We first show by induction that for allt, one has|$M_t = C_t = s_i$|⁠. Fort= 0 we know that in equilibrium the total amount of capital invested in sectoriand the total mass of entrepreneurs in sectoriare equal toKi, thus|$C_0 = M_0 = K_i$|⁠. At the end of the first period the mass of capital not investedC1is equal to the socially responsible capital that was matched with non-complying entrepreneurs, and is|$C_1 = s_i C_0 n_0$|⁠. The mass of entrepreneurs not financed equals to the non-complying entrepreneurs matched with socially responsible capital and equals|$M_1 = n_0 M_0 s_i = C_1$|⁠. Note that at the end of the first round all compliant entrepreneurs have been financed, thus if|$n_0 = 0$|⁠, then for allt> 0,|$M_t = C_t = n_t = 0$|and the matching process only lasts one round. If|$n_0 \gt 0$|then for allt> 0, we havent= 1. In this case we can apply the same argument for a general periodtto have that ifMt=Ct, and|$M_{t + 1} = C_{t + 1} = s_i C_t$|⁠. In periodta non-compliant entrepreneur is financed only if she is matched with non-socially responsible capital and this occurs with probability|${{C_t - s_i C_t } \over {M_t }} = 1 - s_i$|becauseCt=Mt. □

Consider a compliant entrepreneur. She will be funded in the first period and exit the matching market. Her revenue will be |$V_{ic} = \pi _{iC} \lambda$|⁠ , where π iC is the profit of compliant firm in sector i . Non-compliant entrepreneurs can only be financed with non-socially responsible capital. From the above lemma, as long as a non-complying entrepreneur has not been financed, her expected payoff is $$V_{in} = (1 - s_i )\lambda \pi _{iN} + s_i \eta _i V_{in} .$$

That is, with probability if the |$(1 - s_i )$|⁠ , the entrepreneur is matched with non-socially responsible capital. She starts the firm immediately and retains a fraction λ of a non-compliant firm profit π iN . With probability s i the entrepreneur faces a socially responsible capital provider, she is not financed and has to go back to the matching market that will provide with a new capital provider with probability η i . This implies $$V_{in} = \lambda \pi _{iN} \left( {{{1 - s_i } \over {1 - \eta _i s_i }}} \right).$$

The entrepreneur will choose a compliant technology only if |$V_{in} \le V_{ic}$|⁠ , that is $$\pi _{iN} \underbrace {\left( {{{1 - s_i } \over {1 - \eta _i s_i }}} \right)}_{\phi _i (s_i )} \le \pi _{iC} .$$

That corresponds to condition(15). Note that in equilibrium the SRF chooses its portfolio and the emission caps as to induce all entrepreneurs to comply. Thus all entrepreneurs are matched in the very first round of the our search game, that hence only lasts one period.
